sync_vs_async.py - reference impl. All numbers below feed SYNC_VS_ASYNC.md.
Python stdlib only. Deterministic inputs. (Delta=1.0s, GST=6.0s, mean_RTT=50ms)

========================================================================
  SECTION A: SYNCHRONOUS MODEL - detection time = 2*Delta  (GOLD)
========================================================================
Three nodes n0, n1, n2. One-way message delay bound Delta = 1.0s.
So a round trip (ping out + reply back) is bounded by 2*Delta = 2.0s.

FAILURE-DETECTION RULE (synchronous):
  n0 pings n2 at time t. If n2 is alive, its reply arrives at n0
  by t + 2*Delta = t + 2.0s.
  If NO reply by t + 2*Delta, n2 MUST be crashed - the sync bound
  forbids any other explanation (no 'maybe it is slow' excuse).

DEMO: n0 pings n2 every 1.0s. n2 CRASHES at t = 2.5s.

Ping/reply timeline (n0's view). 'reply by' = sent + 2*Delta:
| ping# | sent at | arrives n2 | reply by | outcome                        |
|-------|----------|------------|----------|--------------------------------|
| 0     | 0.0      | 1.0        | 2.0      | n2 alive -> reply arrives OK
| 1     | 1.0      | 2.0        | 3.0      | n2 alive -> reply arrives OK
| 2     | 2.0      | 3.0        | 4.0      | ping arrives AFTER crash -> NO reply -> DEAD @ t=4.0

Detection happens on ping #2: sent @ t=2.0s, declared DEAD @ t=4.0s.
  detection_time = reply_by - sent = 4.0 - 2.0 = 2.0s = 2*Delta.
  (wall-clock detection since the crash @ 2.5s is 1.5s - faster than 2*Delta here, because the
   detecting ping was already in flight when n2 died.)

GOLD: synchronous failure-detection latency (the silence window) = 2*Delta = 2.0s.
[check] detection_time == 2*Delta == 2.0s:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION B: ASYNCHRONOUS MODEL - 'slow' vs 'crashed' is UNKNOWABLE
========================================================================
Same three nodes, but now the network gives NO delay guarantee.

Two runs. SAME observer (n0). SAME observation. DIFFERENT truth:

n0 sends n2 a probe @ t=0 and waits T = 5.0s.
At t = 5.0s n0 has received NO reply. Two worlds fit the
observation equally well:

| world        | truth                        | n0 sees @ t=5.0 |
|--------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| RUN 1: SLOW  | n2 ALIVE, reply delayed 10s  | no reply yet    |
| RUN 2: CRASH | n2 DEAD since t=0            | no reply yet    |

The observations are byte-identical, so n0 CANNOT tell which world
it is in. Whatever timeout T the protocol picks, the adversary picks
a delay just over T and forces a wrong call:

| chosen timeout T | adversary reply delay | n0 verdict @ T | correct?            |
|------------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1.0              | 1.001                  | dead @ t=  1.0 | WRONG (n2 alive) |
| 2.0              | 2.001                  | dead @ t=  2.0 | WRONG (n2 alive) |
| 5.0              | 5.001                  | dead @ t=  5.0 | WRONG (n2 alive) |
| 10.0             | 10.001                 | dead @ t= 10.0 | WRONG (n2 alive) |
| 100.0            | 100.001                | dead @ t=100.0 | WRONG (n2 alive) |

For EVERY finite T the adversary sets delay = T + epsilon, making n0
falsely declare n2 dead. No finite timeout wins -> there is NO
correct failure detector in the pure async model. This is the seed
of FLP (Section C).

[check] for all tried T, adversary delay T+epsilon fools the detector:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION C: FLP - bivalent states stay bivalent forever
========================================================================
CONSENSUS (binary): each process proposes 0 or 1; all non-faulty
processes must decide the SAME value (AGREEMENT), eventually
(TERMINATION), choosing a value that was proposed (VALIDITY).

Configuration valency:
  0-VALENT : from here, EVERY execution decides 0.
  1-VALENT : from here, EVERY execution decides 1.
  BIVALENT : BOTH 0 and 1 are still reachable (undecided).

LEMMA 1 (a bivalent initial configuration exists):
  Compare two starts: (all propose 0) -> validity forces decision 0;
  (all propose 1) -> validity forces decision 1. Walk from one start
  to the other, changing one proposal at a time. The forced decision
  must FLIP somewhere on this walk, so the crossing configuration is
  BIVALENT. Hence a bivalent initial state exists.

LEMMA 2 (bivalence can be preserved forever) - toy illustration:
  Toy protocol: 2 processes p0, p1. p0 proposes 0, p1 proposes 1.
  Rule: the FIRST process to RECEIVE the other's proposal decides
  that value and broadcasts DECIDE(v); all then decide v.

  Two messages are in flight:
    m0 = (p0 -> p1, value 0)   m1 = (p1 -> p0, value 1)
    - deliver m0 first -> p1 receives 0 -> decides 0 -> all decide 0.
    - deliver m1 first -> p0 receives 1 -> decides 1 -> all decide 1.
  BOTH futures are reachable, so the initial state is BIVALENT.

ADVERSARY'S BIVALENT CHAIN (never deliver a 'deciding' message):
| step | action by adversary              | m0 in flight | m1 in flight | 0 reachable | 1 reachable | valency  |
|------|----------------------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| 0    | initial state (both msgs sent)   | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |
| 1    | delay BOTH m0 and m1 another epsilon | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |
| 2    | delay BOTH m0 and m1 another epsilon | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |
| 3    | delay BOTH m0 and m1 another epsilon | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |
| 4    | delay BOTH m0 and m1 another epsilon | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |
| 5    | delay BOTH m0 and m1 another epsilon | yes          | yes          | yes         | yes         | BIVALENT |

At EVERY step the adversary keeps both messages in flight, so both
the 0-deciding and 1-deciding futures remain reachable. The protocol
can NEVER decide -> TERMINATION is violated. Repeated indefinitely
this is an infinite bivalent chain -> no deterministic async
consensus protocol can guarantee termination with even 1 crash
(FLP, Fischer-Lynch-Paterson 1985).

NOTE: the full FLP proof handles arbitrarily many in-flight events
and shows that for any 'critical' event e whose delivery would force
a decision, a finite prelude of OTHER deliveries leaves the post-e
configuration STILL bivalent. The toy above is the load-bearing
intuition; the real proof generalizes it to any protocol.

[check] bivalent steps demonstrable = unbounded (>= 6); FLP holds:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION D: PARTIAL SYNCHRONY - 'eventually synchronous' (DLS 1988)
========================================================================
Dwork-Lynch-Stockmeyer (1988) define THREE timing models:

  (1) SYNCHRONOUS     : Delta known up front. Consensus solvable with
                        f < n crash faults.
  (2) ASYNCHRONOUS    : no Delta. FLP -> unsolvable with even 1 crash.
  (3) PARTIAL SYNC    : a bound Delta EXISTS, and there is a time GST
                        after which delays are <= Delta. GST and Delta
                        are UNKNOWN to the protocol. Consensus IS
                        solvable with f < n/2 crash faults.

Partial synchrony is the model real systems (Raft, Paxos) assume.

DEMO timeline (GST = 6.0s, Delta_after = 1.0s):

| t    | event              | delay  | phase                | outcome                       |
|------|--------------------|--------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1.0  | leader A elected   | 0.6s   | before GST (async-like) | 0.6s < any timeout -> A looks fine |
| 2.0  | A heartbeat        | 9.0s   | before GST (async-like) | 9s > timeout -> A deposed |
| 3.5  | leader B elected   | 4.0s   | before GST (async-like) | 4s > timeout -> B deposed too |
| 6.0  | <<< GST >>>        | -      | after GST (sync-like) | network stabilizes here |
| 6.5  | leader C elected   | 0.8s   | after GST (sync-like) | 0.8s < Delta -> C survives |
| 7.5  | C heartbeat        | 0.9s   | after GST (sync-like) | 0.9s < Delta -> stable |
| 8.5  | C heartbeat        | 1.0s   | after GST (sync-like) | = Delta -> still within bound |

Before GST, leaders keep getting deposed: their heartbeats exceed the
timeout because delays are unbounded. After GST, delays fall within
Delta, so leader C's heartbeats always arrive in time -> C stays
leader -> consensus proceeds. The protocol did NOT need to KNOW GST;
it just kept re-electing until the network stabilized.

This is EXACTLY how Raft and Paxos work: they assume partial
synchrony, use timeouts as a best guess for Delta, and make progress
once GST has passed. (🔗 Raft: randomized 150-300ms election timeout;
see Section E.)

[check] leaders churned before GST = 2 (>1); stable leader after GST = 1:  OK

========================================================================
  SECTION E: TIMEOUT TRADE-OFF - false positives vs failover latency
========================================================================
Raft randomized election timeout: 150-300ms. Paxos: similar.

Model a heartbeat's network delay as EXPONENTIAL with mean mu = 50ms (standard queueing-delay model).
  P(delay > T) = exp(-T / mu).
A follower starts an election if its election timeout T_elapses with
no heartbeat. So P(false election) = P(heartbeat delay > T). Failover
latency on a REAL crash ~= T (you wait T, then elect).

Trade-off table (per election-term window):

| T_elect | P(false election) = e^(-T/mu) | failover (real crash) | verdict                      |
|---------|------------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
|   50ms  | 36.7879%                     |      50 ms             | too short -> many false elections |
|  100ms  | 13.5335%                     |     100 ms             | too short -> many false elections |
|  150ms  | 4.9787%                      |     150 ms             | Raft sweet spot 150-300ms |
|  300ms  | 0.2479%                      |     300 ms             | Raft sweet spot 150-300ms |
|  500ms  | 0.0045%                      |     500 ms             | too long -> slow failover |
| 1000ms  | 0.0000%                      |    1000 ms             | too long -> slow failover |

Reading the table:
  - T=50ms : 36.79% of terms are FALSE elections - constant
             leader churn, the cluster never settles.
  - T=150ms: 4.98% false elections - tolerable; failover
             ~150ms - snappy. Raft's lower bound.
  - T=300ms: 0.2479% false elections - very stable; failover
             ~300ms. Raft's upper bound.
  - T=1000ms: ~0% false elections, but a real crash takes ~1s to
             heal - users notice the outage.

Raft randomizes T per node across 150-300ms to avoid split votes
(two followers timing out simultaneously). The window is the partial-
synchrony compromise: low enough for quick failover, high enough to
ride out normal network jitter.

[check] P(false) decreases monotonically with T: True; failover increases monotonically: True:  OK

========================================================================
  DONE - all sections printed
========================================================================
